No; in FY2022, 3M’s purchases of property, plant and equipment were $1.749 billion versus net sales of $34.229 billion (about 5.1%), and net property, plant and equipment was $9.178 billion.

3M’s operating income margin decreased to 19.1% in 2022 from 20.8% in 2021; excluding special items, it fell to 20.8% from 22.2%, driven mainly by a 2.4-point raw material impact from inflationary increases in raw material and logistics costs, partly offset by a 1.0-point benefit from organic growth/productivity and other factors. Organic growth/productivity was helped by strong pricing, spending discipline and 2021 restructuring actions, but was hurt by a 0.3-point decline from lower disposable respirator demand, manufacturing headwinds from global supply chain challenges, geopolitical impacts from the Russia/Ukraine conflict, ongoing COVID-related challenges in China, and increased investments in growth, productivity and sustainability. Special items also reduced 2022 margin, including net costs for significant litigation, divestiture costs, Russia exit charges and PFAS manufacturing exit costs, partly offset by the gain on the Food Safety business divestiture.
Consumer.

3M’s quick ratio at June 30, 2023 was approximately 0.85, calculated as ($4,258 million cash and cash equivalents + $56 million current marketable securities + $4,947 million accounts receivable) divided by $10,936 million of current liabilities. While quick assets were less than current liabilities, the filing states that “3M maintains a strong liquidity profile,” supported by cash on hand, access to the commercial paper market, and an undrawn $4.25 billion revolving credit facility.
1.500% Notes due 2026 (MMM26), 1.750% Notes due 2030 (MMM30), and 1.500% Notes due 2031 (MMM31), each registered on the New York Stock Exchange.

Yes. 3M declared and paid $1.50 per share in each of the first and second quarters of 2023 versus $1.49 per share in each of the comparable 2022 quarters, and it stated this marked its 65th consecutive year of dividend increases.
Adobe’s unadjusted operating income increased by 65.4% from FY2015 to FY2016, calculated as (($1,493,602 - $903,095) / $903,095) × 100 = 65.4%.

No. Adobe’s operating margin was about 34.6% in FY2022 ($6.10 billion operating income on $17.61 billion revenue), down from about 36.8% in FY2021, although still above about 32.9% in FY2020, so its operating margin profile was not improving in FY2022.

Yes. Based on the document, free cash flow (cash flows from operations less purchases of property and equipment) was approximately $7.40 billion in FY2022 versus $6.88 billion in FY2021, and relative to net income this implies conversion improved to about 156% in FY2022 from about 143% in FY2021.

Using total cost of sales of $10,069 million and average inventory of $829.5 million (($1,055 million + $604 million) / 2), AES’s FY2022 inventory turnover ratio was about 12.1x.  
However, conventional inventory turnover is of limited meaning for AES because it is primarily a power generation and utility company that sells electricity, and its inventory “primarily consists of fuel and other raw materials used to generate power, and operational spare parts and supplies used to maintain power generation and distribution facilities,” rather than merchandise held for resale.

30.8% increase
Amcor’s FY2020 year-end net AR was $1,615.9 million, based on the balance sheet line item “Trade receivables, net” as of June 30, 2020.

The key agenda was the execution of supplemental indentures relating to the substitution of Amcor Flexibles North America, Inc. for Amcor Finance (USA), Inc. as issuer under the indentures governing the 3.625% Guaranteed Senior Notes due 2026 and the 4.500% Guaranteed Senior Notes due 2028, and the assumption by the substitute issuer of the former issuer’s covenants under those indentures.

AMCOR’s quick ratio improved between FY2022 and FY2023. Using cash and cash equivalents plus trade receivables divided by total current liabilities, it was about 0.57 in FY2023 (($689+$1,875)/$4,476) versus about 0.53 in FY2022 (($775+$1,935)/$5,103).

FY2023: Amcor completed three acquisitions—100% of a Czech Republic company operating a flexible packaging manufacturing plant (August 1, 2022), 100% of a medical device packaging manufacturing site in Shanghai, China (March 17, 2023), and a New Zealand-based manufacturer of automated protein packaging machines (May 31, 2023).  
FY2022: no acquisitions were disclosed.  
FY2021: no acquisitions were disclosed.
The packaging industry.

No. Amcor’s gross profit as a percentage of net sales declined to 18.5% in FY2023 from 19.4% in FY2022, and the company said the decrease was mainly due to the pass-through of higher raw material costs and the impact of disposed operations. Gross margin is therefore a somewhat limited metric here because price pass-through of raw material costs can distort the percentage even when underlying gross profit performance improved, as Amcor noted that excluding currency impacts and disposed/ceased operations, gross profit increased by $56 million.

As of December 31, 2022, Amcor’s restructuring liability was a $93 million accrual for employee costs ($81 million), fixed asset-related costs ($2 million), and other costs ($10 million), primarily related to restructuring activities associated with the Russia-Ukraine conflict. It is intended to cover these restructuring obligations, with the majority expected to be paid within the next 12 months.
$2,018 million.
0% — net sales on a comparable constant currency basis were in line with the prior year.
